mappings.rationaleAI: Psychometric measurement including IQ, Wechsler scales, and Raven's Progressive Matrices directly addresses types of testing instruments (B.1), development of standardized tests (B.2), and assessment of test results (B.3). Theories of cognitive abilities relate to intellectual abilities (D). Coverage of the Flynn effect, sex differences, and cognitive epidemiology addresses intelligence standards (E.1), retardation (E.2), the gifted (E.3), and group differences (E.4).

Broad coverage of cognitive psychology, developmental psychology, and individual differences provides context for human capacities (A), intellectual abilities (D), personal propensities (F), and attitudes (G).
Cognitive psychology of reasoning, dual-process theory, and the relationship between IQ and reasoning ability directly addresses theories of intelligence (D).
Neural basis of motor action addresses sensorimotor abilities (C) and intellectual abilities through neural substrates of cognition (D).
Cognitive development from birth through adolescence addresses the nature of human capacities (A). Moral reasoning relates to the development of attitudes (G.3).
Adolescent cognitive maturation and identity formation address developing human capacities (A). Attitudinal changes relate to the development of attitudes (G.3).
Analysis of genius examining talent, intelligence, and creativity directly addresses theories of intelligence (D) and the gifted (E.3).
Psychological models of creative expression address intellectual ability (D), the gifted (E.3), and personal propensities (F).
Theory of mind deficit and executive function challenges address atypical cognitive profiles (E.2). Restricted interests relate to personal propensities (F).
Reframing neurological variation addresses intelligence standards (E.1). Cognitive differences relate to personal propensities (F).
Relationship between heredity, intelligence, and race directly addresses group differences in intelligence across race and other factors (E.4).
Biological racism relates to group differences in intelligence across race (E.4). Implicit bias addresses the nature of attitudes (G.1).
Inherited traits shaping understandings of intelligence and race directly addresses group differences and their interpretation (E.4).
Statistical methods provide the mathematical foundation for testing instruments (B.1) and assessment of results (B.3).
Theory of measurement in behavioural sciences addresses psychological measurement (B), testing instruments (B.1), and assessment (B.3).
Prejudice, attitudes, and persuasion address the nature of attitudes (G.1) and functions of attitudes (G.2).
Temperament and biological basis of emotions relate to personal propensities and idiosyncrasies (F).
Gender inequality analysis relates to group differences and sex-based differences in intelligence (E.4).
Associative theories of conditioning provide foundational context for the nature of human capacities (A).
